params.text:
What the law and the regulator expect

Record keeping is not just good practice — it is a legal duty. In England, the Care Quality Commission (CQC) inspects it under Regulation 17: Good Governance of the Health and Social Care Act 2008 (Regulated Activities) Regulations 2014. Providers must keep, securely, an accurate, complete and contemporaneous record for each person, including a record of the care and support provided.

  • Records must be accurate, complete and contemporaneous (written at, or close to, the time).
  • They must be kept secure and only accessed, amended or destroyed by people authorised to do so.
  • They must be available to the right people when needed to deliver safe care.
  • They must be created, stored and disposed of in line with current law and recognised guidance.

Skills for Care and social care recording guidance add that entries should be person-centred, clearly separate facts from opinions, and keep jargon and abbreviations to a minimum so anyone reading them can understand.

params.question: Which CQC regulation covers record keeping and the good governance of a care service?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Regulation 17 requires an accurate, complete and contemporaneous record for each person, kept securely.
Regulation 12 is about safe care and treatment. Record keeping and governance sit under Regulation 17.
Regulation 9 concerns person-centred care. The good-governance and records duty is Regulation 17.
Regulation 13 covers safeguarding. Governance and record keeping are set out in Regulation 17.
